finding.contract_claim: getNodeLabel accesses node.children (line 18) and node.size/node.extension (line 21) on a value typed FsNode = FileNode | DirectoryNode, and each property exists on only one union member — accessing the wrong variant would yield undefined at runtime.
finding.observed_behavior: Each access is guarded by a discriminant check on node.type (line 17): the `directory` branch reads .children and the post-guard fall-through is narrowed to FileNode before reading .size/.extension.
finding.triage_reasoning: The claim alleges that accessing `node.children` and `node.size`/`node.extension` on the union type `FsNode = FileNode | DirectoryNode` could yield `undefined` at runtime. However, the code correctly uses TypeScript discriminant narrowing on `node.type`: the `if (node.type === "directory")` guard on line 17 narrows to `DirectoryNode` before accessing `.children`, and TypeScript further narrows the fallthrough to `FileNode` before accessing `.size`/`.extension`. The comment on line 20 even documents this explicitly. Both branches are type-safe. The gap does not exist.
